slides.setup_instructions: Place the sandpaper letter H and basket of /h/ objects on the work mat, ensuring good lighting for clear visibility.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special rough letter H and all these fun things that start with the /h/ sound! Can you guess which sound we'll be learning about today?

slides.text: Trace the sandpaper letter H with your index and middle fingers while saying the /h/ sound.
slides.mascot_explanation: Use two fingers like I use my claws to trace! Go down, across, then down again while making the /h/ sound. Feel how rough and bumpy the letter is!

slides.text: Explore each object while emphasizing the beginning /h/ sound: hat-/h/, house-/h/, heart-/h/.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's look at each special thing! Can you hear that /h/ sound at the very beginning of each word? Hat, house, heart - they all start the same way!

slides.prompt: Play 'I Spy' with the /h/ sound using the objects and other items around the room that begin with /h/. Take turns tracing the letter H between each round.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we'll play my favorite game - I Spy! I spy with my little eye something that starts with /h/. Can you find it and trace the letter H after you guess?

items: Can you trace the letter H in the correct direction?
Do you make the /h/ sound when tracing?
Can you identify objects that begin with the /h/ sound?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let me see how well you know the /h/ sound! Show me your best letter H tracing and find something else in our room that starts with /h/.

slides.text: Trace the letter H one final time while saying the /h/ sound. Put away the materials carefully and remember the /h/ sound for next time.
slides.mastery_check: Child can trace letter H correctly and identify at least 2-3 objects beginning with /h/ sound.
slides.mascot_explanation: Wonderful work learning about H and the /h/ sound! Give that letter one more special trace with your fingers, and remember - H says /h/ like hat and house.

title: Sandpaper Numeral 8 - Tracing and Quantity Recognition
description: Children explore the sandpaper numeral 8 through tactile tracing while practicing one-to-one correspondence with eight objects. This lesson reinforces number recognition and quantity understanding through multi-sensory learning.
instructions: 1. Prepare the sandpaper numeral 8 on a tray with a bowl containing exactly 8 small counting objects (beans, buttons, or shells).
2. Sit beside your child and present the numeral 8, saying 'This is eight' while tracing it with your index and middle fingers.
3. Demonstrate the proper tracing pattern: start at the top, curve around and cross in the middle, then curve around the bottom.
4. Invite your child to trace the numeral 8 while saying 'eight', guiding their fingers gently if needed.
5. Count out eight objects from the bowl, placing them in a line while counting aloud: 'one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight.'
6. Have your child count the objects again, touching each one as they count to reinforce one-to-one correspondence.
7. Mix up the eight objects and invite your child to arrange them in different patterns (circle, square, line) while counting.
8. End the lesson by tracing the numeral 8 one more time and counting the eight objects together.
materials_needed: Bowl with 8 counting objects (beans, buttons, shells)
parent_notes: The sandpaper numerals help children internalize number symbols through their sense of touch, which supports long-term retention. At this age, focus on helping your child understand that '8' represents a specific quantity of objects. The tracing motion should be slow and deliberate - this builds muscle memory for later writing. If your child struggles with counting to 8, break it into smaller groups (4+4) to make it more manageable.

slides.heading: Sandpaper Numeral 8 - Tracing and Quantity Recognition
slides.subheading: Discovering the number 8 through touch and counting
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm so excited to explore the number 8 with you today! We'll trace it with our fingers and count eight special objects together.

slides.setup_instructions: Place the sandpaper numeral 8 and bowl of exactly 8 objects on the work mat with good lighting.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special rough number 8 and this bowl with exactly 8 counting treasures! The number feels bumpy when we touch it, just like the bark on my favorite tree.

slides.text: Trace the sandpaper numeral 8 with your index and middle fingers, starting at the top and making two connected circles.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's trace the number 8 together! It looks like two circles stacked on top of each other, or like a snowman lying down. Start at the top and trace around!

slides.text: Count out eight objects from the bowl, placing them in a line and touching each one as you count aloud.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's count our eight special objects! Touch each one as you count - one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight! That's exactly what the number 8 means.

slides.prompt: Arrange the eight objects in different patterns (line, circle, square) while counting them each time to confirm there are still eight objects.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is fun! Let's move our eight objects into different shapes. Can you make a circle? A straight line? No matter how we arrange them, we still have eight!

items: Can you trace the numeral 8 correctly from top to bottom?
Can you count to 8 touching each object once?
Do you understand that 8 objects is always 8 regardless of arrangement?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let me see how well you know the number 8! Show me your best tracing and count those objects one more time. You're becoming a real number expert!

slides.text: Trace the numeral 8 one final time and count your eight objects together. Put the materials away carefully and remember that 8 represents eight things.
slides.mastery_check: Child can trace numeral 8 correctly and count eight objects using one-to-one correspondence.
slides.mascot_explanation: Excellent work with the number 8 today! Give that bumpy numeral one more trace and let's count our eight objects one last time. Eight is such a special number!

title: Playdough Spring Garden Sculptures
description: Children create three-dimensional spring garden scenes using playdough, developing fine motor skills and creative expression. This sculptural activity encourages imaginative play while building hand strength and artistic confidence.
instructions: 1. Prepare different colored playdough (green, yellow, pink, brown) and simple tools like plastic knives, rolling pins, and cookie cutters.
2. Show your child how to knead the playdough to make it soft and workable.
3. Demonstrate basic techniques: rolling snakes for worms, flattening circles for flowers, and pinching pieces for petals.
4. Encourage your child to create a spring garden scene with flowers, grass, trees, or garden creatures.
5. Model how to join pieces by pressing gently and smoothing edges together.
6. Support your child's creative choices, asking open-ended questions like 'Tell me about your garden' or 'What grows here?'
7. Allow your child to work at their own pace, offering help only when requested.
8. Display the finished garden sculpture in a special place and take a photo to preserve their artwork.
materials_needed: Plastic rolling pins or cylindrical objects
parent_notes: Playdough work strengthens the small muscles in children's hands, which is essential preparation for writing. Allow your child to explore freely rather than expecting a specific outcome - the process is more important than the product. This type of open-ended artistic expression builds confidence and creative thinking skills. If your child becomes frustrated, offer gentle assistance but avoid taking over their work.

slides.heading: Playdough Spring Garden Sculptures
slides.subheading: Creating colorful gardens with our hands
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello creative friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I love making things with my paws! Today we're going to squish and shape playdough into beautiful spring gardens full of flowers and fun creatures.

slides.setup_instructions: Set up all materials on the work mat with playdough containers open and tools easily accessible.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all our wonderful squishy playdough colors! We have tools to help us roll, cut, and shape our garden creations. Everything feels so nice in our hands!

slides.mascot_explanation: First, let's wake up our playdough! Squeeze it and press it with your hands, just like I knead the soft moss in the forest. Feel how it gets softer and more squishy!

slides.text: Practice basic techniques: roll snakes, flatten circles, and pinch small pieces for details like flower petals.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's learn some special playdough tricks! Roll it like a snake, pat it flat like a pancake, and pinch tiny pieces for flower petals. These will help make our garden beautiful!

slides.prompt: Create your own spring garden scene using different colors and techniques. Make flowers, grass, trees, or any garden creatures you can imagine.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now comes the most fun part - making your very own garden! What will you put in your garden? Flowers? Trees? Maybe some friendly bugs or worms? Let your imagination grow like a beautiful garden!

items: Are you using different colors creatively in your garden?
Can you join pieces together by pressing and smoothing?

slides.text: Share your garden sculpture and tell the story of what you created. Clean up the materials and display your artwork in a special place.
slides.mastery_check: Child can manipulate playdough using basic techniques and create a recognizable garden scene.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful garden you made! I can see flowers and so many wonderful details. Let's put your garden somewhere special so everyone can admire your amazing artwork!

title: We're Going on a Bear Hunt - Adventure Sound Effects
description: Children enjoy the repetitive, adventurous story while participating in sound effects and movements that correspond to different terrain and obstacles. This interactive reading builds phonological awareness and gross motor coordination.
instructions: 1. Gather your child in a comfortable reading spot with the book 'We're Going on a Bear Hunt' visible.
2. Introduce the book by showing the cover and asking your child to predict what adventure they think will happen.
3. Begin reading slowly, emphasizing the repetitive phrases and encouraging your child to join in when they feel comfortable.
4. Pause at each terrain description (grass, mud, forest, etc.) and make the corresponding sound effects together.
5. Add simple movements like marching in place, tiptoeing, or swimming motions to match the story's actions.
6. Build suspense during the bear cave scene by reading slowly and quietly, then getting excited during the chase back.
7. Encourage your child to make the swishy-swashy, squelch-squelch, and other onomatopoeia sounds throughout.
8. After reading, discuss which part was most exciting and act out the favorite scene together.
materials_needed: We're Going on a Bear Hunt book
Optional: simple props like scarves or stuffed animals
parent_notes: This book's repetitive structure and sound effects make it perfect for building phonological awareness and language prediction skills. The interactive nature keeps young children engaged while developing listening skills and story comprehension. Don't worry if your child wants to hear it multiple times - repetition is how young children learn and find comfort in stories. The adventure theme often appeals to children who enjoy active, exploratory play.

slides.heading: We're Going on a Bear Hunt - Adventure Sound Effects
slides.subheading: An exciting journey with sounds and movement
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi brave adventurers! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm so excited to go on a bear hunt with you! We'll make all the sounds and movements as we go through grass, mud, and more on our adventure!

items: We're Going on a Bear Hunt book

slides.prompt: Read the story with interactive sound effects and movements for each terrain (swishy-swashy grass, squelch-squelch mud, etc.). Act out the adventure together.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now for the fun part! We'll make swishy-swashy sounds in the grass, squelch-squelch sounds in the mud, and march like we're really on the adventure! Follow along with me!
